# Relative color syntax

<FeatureLinks mdn="https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/search?q=%E7%9B%B8%E5%AF%B9%E9%A2%9C%E8%89%B2%E8%AF%AD%E6%B3%95%20CSS" caniuse="https://caniuse.com/?search=%E7%9B%B8%E5%AF%B9%E9%A2%9C%E8%89%B2%E8%AF%AD%E6%B3%95" status="Color 5 / Snapshot pre-CR snapshot, publishable" label="Reference links" />

## Plain-language explanation

Extract channels from an existing color and derive new colors.

## Representative syntax

```css
oklch(from var(--brand) l c calc(h + 30))
```

## Demo

<CssDemo title="Relative color syntax" css-b64="" code-b64="" html-b64="" caption="The important part is the relationship between colors: one semantic color generates a maintainable set of states." lang="en-US" badge="Practical demo" />

## When to use it

<div class="usage-grid">
<section>
  <h3>Best fit</h3>
  <p>Use Relative color syntax in theme and state systems when you need to build color variations from semantic inputs instead of hand-tuning every state. It usually gives the stylesheet a clearer job than extra classes, wrappers, or script.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Example</h3>
  <p>A warning banner can derive its background, border, and text color from one token and still adapt in dark mode.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Watch out</h3>
  <p>Perceptual spaces help with ramps, but they do not replace contrast checks against real text sizes and backgrounds.</p>
</section>
</div>
